text: The surgeon opens the peritoneal leaf of the transverse mesocolon medially, exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon opens the epiplume retrocavity, facilitating the dissection of the mesocolon from the body and tail of the pancreas due to the absence of the left branch of the middle colic artery.
The surgeon continues etching the peritoneal leaf of the left mesocolon in a caudal direction, progressing towards the identification of the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon performs vascular dissection, revealing the left colic vein. The vein is separated from the left colic artery and sealed at its confluence with the anterior mesenteric vein using a radiofrequency device.
The surgeon sections the left colic artery, completing the vascular dissection in the area.
The surgeon etches the peritoneal leaf of the mesosigma and proximal rectum, progressing from cranial to caudal direction, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon begins vascular dissection posterior to the inferior mesenteric artery, proceeding along the inferior mesenteric vein. The vein is divided from the left colic artery, which is visible running behind it.
The surgeon carefully preserves the hypogastric plexus while dissecting around the inferior mesenteric artery, ensuring the nerve structure remains intact during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the vascular dissection, revealing the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery from the aorta and the bifurcation of the left colic artery from the main pedicle.
The surgeon identifies and sections the left colic vein, which descends from the splenic flexure to its confluence with the inferior mesenteric veins.
The surgeon sections the inferior mesenteric vein, superior rectal artery, and sigmoid artery using the low-tie technique, without employing clips or suture devices.
